clipboardHistoryRecords.textContent:
#!/bin/bash

# Check for correct number of arguments
if [ "$#" -ne 2 ]; then
  echo "Usage: $0 <input_rayconfig_file> <password>"
  exit 1
fi

INPUT_FILE="$1"
PASSWORD="$2"
OUTPUT_FILE="$(dirname "$INPUT_FILE")/$(basename "$INPUT_FILE" .rayconfig).json"

echo "Attempting to decrypt '$INPUT_FILE' with provided password..."

# Decrypt and decompress the file
openssl enc -d -aes-256-cbc -nosalt -in "$INPUT_FILE" -k "$PASSWORD" 2>/dev/null | tail -c +17 | gunzip > "$OUTPUT_FILE"

if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then
  echo "Decryption and decompression successful! Output saved to '$OUTPUT_FILE'"
else
  echo "Decryption or decompression failed. Please check the password and input file."
fi

to Raycast Notes

Thank you for checking out Raycast Notes, a handy small scratchpad for your new ideas, meeting notes, and things you want to jot down quickly.



📑 Browsing Notes

You can find your notes with ⌘ P or via the panel button in the title bar. You can have up to 5 notes on the free plan, and an unlimited amount with Raycast Pro.

📏 Window Resizing

Raycast Notes’ height is dynamic by default. You can override this by simply re-sizing it manually.

To get back to auto-sizing, you can hover around the bottom edge, where you will see an “auto-size” button appear.

⚡ Actions

You can find some helpful actions from the ⌘ K menu or the ⌘ icon in the title bar.

🎨 Formatting

You can use markdown to format your text, using # for headings, **bold**, [] for task items and so on. Check out the full list here.



We hope you enjoy using Raycast Notes. Please feel free to leave us feedback in our Slack Community clipboardHistoryRecords.textContent:
# Jerry

Jerry is the main Elixir app written with Phoenix and Phoenix LiveView.

## How to run

1. `docker compose up -d` if the Docker containers are not running.
2. Start Phoenix server with `make run` or `make replserver` with interactive mode.


### EntryPoints

| EntryPoint               | URL                                         |
| ------------------------ | --------------------------------------------|
| JerryWeb.Endpoint        | [`localhost:4000`](http://localhost:4000)   |
| ApplicationFlow.Endpoint | [`localhost:4002`](http://localhost:4002)   |
| Playground.Endpoint      | [`localhost:4005`](http://localhost:4005)   |
| Api.Endpoint             | [`localhost:4010`](http://localhost:4010)   |


Ready to run in production? Please [check our deployment guides](https://hexdocs.pm/phoenix/deployment.html).


### Environment

| Environment | What                     | URL                                         |
| ----------- | ------------------------ | --------------------------------------------|
| Acceptance  | Dashboard                | http://jerry.acceptance.equalture.com/      |
| Acceptance  | Application flow         | https://app.acceptance.equalture.com/       |
| Acceptance  | Games                    | http://playground.acceptance.equalture.com  |
| Acceptance  | API                      | https://api.acceptance.equalture.com        |
| Demo        | Dashboard                | https://jerry.demo.equalture.com            |
| Production  | Dashboard                | https://dashboard.equalture.com/            |


### Tests

- To setup a test database with eventstore: `make setup-test`
- To run Jerry tests excluding the slowest tests: `make run-test`
- To run all tests: `make run-all-tests`


### Translations

To extract translations from Phoenix run:
`mix gettext.extract`

To upload translations to POEditor tool:
`POEDITOR_API_TOKEN=PASTE_YOUR_POEDITOR_TOKEN make upload-translations-jerry-private`

To download translations from POEditor tool (Dashboard translations):
`POEDITOR_API_TOKEN=PASTE_YOUR_POEDITOR_TOKEN make download-translations project_name=jerry_priv language=en`

To download all translations from POEditor tool:
`POEDITOR_API_TOKEN=PASTE_YOUR_POEDITOR_TOKEN make download-all-translations`


### How to run in Docker

You can run the application locally in Docker with `make run-app-docker`

It is possible to configure if we want to use a remote image or we want to build it from scratch locally. To configure, you should indicate the option in the `docker-compose.app.yml` file in the root directory.

For example:
- `build: components/jerry/.` for building the image locally
- `image: registry.gitlab.com/equalture/equalture/jerry:latest` for using a remote image (authentication to GitLab Container Registry is required).

### Logging in to Gitlab Container Registry

```
docker login -u PERSONAL_TOKEN_FROM_GITLAB registry.gitlab.com
```

clipboardHistoryRecords.text: OpenID Connect (OIDC) is an identity layer built on top of the OAuth 2.0 framework, allowing applications to verify a user's identity via an external Identity Provider (IdP).
OpenID Connect (OIDC) configures authentication by registering a client (app) with a provider (e.g., Azure, Google) to obtain a client ID and secret.

configures authentication by registering a client (app) with a provider (e.g., Azure, Google) to obtain a client ID and secret. Key configuration involves utilizing a /.well-known/openid-configuration URL to automatically discover endpoints, public keys, and supported scopes (like "openid" or "profile") for secure authentication.

Key configuration involves utilizing a /.well-known/openid-configuration URL to automatically discover endpoints, public keys, and supported scopes (like "openid" or "profile") for secure authentication.
Typically https://provider.com/.well-known/openid-configuration. This fetches metadata, including authorization, token, and userinfo endpoints.
The key takeaway from the research
Assent already supports OIDC auto-discovery natively.

The Assent.Strategy.OIDC module accepts a base_url config and fetches the .well-known/openid-configuration document at runtime — so we don't need custom HTTP fetching or endpoint-parsing logic. We just need to pipe the customer's issuer URL into the existing config.

# Get started with GitLab CI/CD

{{< details >}}

- Tier: Free, Premium, Ultimate
- Offering: GitLab.com, GitLab Self-Managed, GitLab Dedicated

{{< /details >}}

CI/CD is a continuous method of software development, where you continuously build,
test, deploy, and monitor iterative code changes.

This iterative process helps reduce the chance that you develop new code based on
buggy or failed previous versions. GitLab CI/CD can catch bugs early in the development cycle,
and help ensure that the code deployed to production complies with your established code standards.

This process is part of a larger workflow:

![GitLab DevSecOps lifecycle with stages for Plan, Create, Verify, Secure, Release, and Monitor.](img/get_started_cicd_v16_11.png)

## Step 1: Configure your pipeline

To use GitLab CI/CD, you start with a `.gitlab-ci.yml` file at the root of your project.
This file specifies the stages, jobs, and scripts to be executed during your CI/CD pipeline.
It is a YAML file with its own custom syntax.

By default, the file is named `.gitlab-ci.yml`, but you can use any filename.

In this file, you define variables, dependencies between jobs, and specify when
and how each job should be executed.

A pipeline is defined in the `.gitlab-ci.yml` file,
and executes when the file runs on a runner.

Pipelines are made up of stages and jobs:

- Stages define the order of execution. Typical stages might be `build`, `test`, and `deploy`.
- Jobs specify the tasks to be performed in each stage. For example, a job can compile or test code.

Pipelines can be triggered by various events, like commits or merges, or can be on schedule.
In your pipeline, you can integrate with a wide range of tools and platforms.

For more information, see:

- [Tutorial: Create and run your first GitLab CI/CD pipeline](quick_start/_index.md)
- [Pipelines](pipelines/_index.md)

## Step 2: Find or create runners

Runners are the agents that run your jobs. These agents can run on physical machines or virtual instances.
In your `.gitlab-ci.yml` file, you can specify a container image you want to use when running the job.
The runner loads the image, clones your project, and runs the job either locally or in the container.

If you use GitLab.com, runners on Linux, Windows, and macOS are already available for use.
If needed, you can also register your own runners.

If you don't use GitLab.com, you can:

- Register runners or use runners already registered for your GitLab Self-Managed instance.
- Create a runner on your local machine.

For more information, see:

- [Create, register, and run your own project runner](../tutorials/create_register_first_runner/_index.md)

## Step 3: Use CI/CD variables and expressions

GitLab CI/CD variables are key-value pairs you use to store and pass configuration settings
and sensitive information, like passwords or API keys, to jobs in a pipeline.

GitLab CI/CD expressions allow you to inject data dynamically into your pipeline configuration.
The data available depends on the expression context.
For example, the `inputs` context allows you to access information passed into the
configuration file from a parent file or when a pipeline is run.

### CI/CD variables

Use CI/CD variables to customize jobs by making values defined elsewhere accessible to jobs.
You can hard-code CI/CD variables in your `.gitlab-ci.yml` file, set them in your project settings,
or generate them dynamically. You can define them for the project, group, or instance.

The following types of variables are available:

- Custom variables: Variables that you create and manage in the UI, API, or configuration files.
- Predefined variables: Variables that GitLab automatically sets to provide information about the current job, pipeline, and environment.

You can configure variables with security settings:

- Protected variables: Restrict access to jobs running on protected branches or tags.
- Masked variables: Hide variable values in job logs to prevent sensitive information from being exposed.

For more information, see:

- [CI/CD variables](variables/_index.md)

### CI/CD expressions

CI/CD expressions use the `$[[ ]]` syntax and are validated when you create a pipeline.
You can also validate expressions in the pipeline editor before committing changes.

Expressions enable dynamic configuration based on different contexts:

- **Inputs context** (`$[[ inputs.INPUT_NAME ]]`): Access typed parameters passed into configuration files with `include:inputs` or when a new pipeline is run
- **Matrix context** (`$[[ matrix.IDENTIFIER ]]`): Access matrix values in job dependencies to create 1:1 mappings between matrix jobs

For more information, see:

- [CI expressions](yaml/expressions.md)

## Step 4: Use CI/CD components

A CI/CD component is a reusable pipeline configuration unit.
Use a CI/CD component to compose an entire pipeline configuration or a small part of a larger pipeline.

You can add a component to your pipeline configuration with `include:component`.

Reusable components help reduce duplication, improve maintainability, and promote consistency across projects.
Create a component project and publish it to the CI/CD Catalog to share your component across multiple projects.

GitLab also has CI/CD component templates for common tasks and integrations.

For more information, see:

- [CI/CD components](components/_index.md)

This document covers the preliminary research for adding a "Custom" OIDC SSO strategy to our platform. Currently, customers must choose from pre-configured providers (Google, Azure AD, etc.) where endpoints are hardcoded in the respective Assent strategy modules. The goal is to let customers provide their own OpenID Connect discovery URL and have our system auto-configure all necessary endpoints, signing keys, and authentication parameters from it.

Key outcome: A customer like CBA can supply a URL such as https://signon.sso.test.cba/identity/.well-known/openid-configuration and our platform will automatically discover and configure the authorization endpoint, token endpoint, JWKS URI, and supported scopes/claims.

Every OpenID Connect-compliant identity provider publishes a JSON document at a standardized URL path (/.well-known/openid-configuration). This document contains all the metadata a relying party needs to integrate: authorization endpoint, token endpoint, JWKS URI, supported scopes, supported signing algorithms, and more.
Instead of hardcoding these values per provider, an application can fetch this single document and extract everything it needs. This is the foundation of OIDC auto-discovery and is defined by the OpenID Connect Discovery 1.0 specification (extended by RFC 8414).
The discovery document is a flat JSON object. The most critical fields for our integration are:
Canonical issuer URL. Must match the iss claim in issued tokens.
URL where users are redirected to authenticate.
URL used to exchange auth codes for tokens.
URL of the JSON Web Key Set for verifying token signatures.
URL to retrieve claims about the authenticated user.
OAuth scopes the provider supports (openid, profile, email, etc.)
How clients authenticate at the token endpoint (e.g. client_secret_basic, private_key_jwt).
Signing algorithms supported for ID tokens (typically RS256).

We currently use Assent v0.3.1 for SSO. Each provider (Google, Azure AD, etc.) is a dedicated module that uses Assent.Strategy.OIDC.Base and hardcodes a base_url in its default_config/1 callback. Assent then appends /.well-known/openid-configuration to that base URL, fetches the discovery document, and uses it to drive the authorize and callback flows.

Key insight: Assent.Strategy.OIDC already supports full OIDC auto-discovery natively. The strategy accepts a base_url config option and fetches the discovery document at runtime. This means we do not need to write custom HTTP fetching or endpoint-parsing logic. We simply need to pass the customer’s issuer URL as the base_url.
The Assent.Strategy.OIDC module accepts several config keys that are relevant to our custom strategy:

base_url (required) — The OIDC issuer URL. Assent appends the well-known path to this.
openid_configuration_uri (optional) — Override the default discovery path. Defaults to /.well-known/openid-configuration.
openid_configuration (optional) — Pass the full discovery document directly (skip the HTTP fetch). Useful for testing or caching.
client_authentication_method (optional) — Defaults to client_secret_basic. Can be: none, client_secret_basic, client_secret_post, client_secret_jwt, private_key_jwt.
id_token_signed_response_alg (optional) — Expected signing algorithm for ID tokens. Defaults to RS256.
trusted_audiences (optional) — List of audiences considered valid when verifying the ID token.

The simplest and most maintainable approach is to use Assent.Strategy.OIDC directly (or wrap it minimally) rather than creating a full custom strategy module. The configuration would be assembled dynamically from the customer’s stored settings:

# Dynamic config built from customer SSO settings:
[
  client_id: customer_sso.client_id,
  client_secret: customer_sso.client_secret,
  base_url: customer_sso.openid_configuration_url |> extract_issuer(),
  redirect_uri: "https://app.example.com/auth/sso/callback",
  authorization_params: [scope: "openid email profile"]
]
|> Assent.Strategy.OIDC.authorize_url()
If we want a cleaner abstraction and the ability to add custom normalization logic, we can define a dedicated module using Assent.Strategy.OIDC.Base:

Recommendation: Start with the direct Assent.Strategy.OIDC approach for the POC. If normalization requirements emerge during testing with real customer IdPs (e.g. CBA), extract into a custom OIDC.Base module.
Handling the Discovery URL vs. Issuer URL
Customers will provide a full discovery URL like:
But Assent expects a base_url (the issuer) and appends the well-known path itself. We have two options:

Option A: Strip the well-known suffix. Parse the provided URL and extract the base (e.g. https://signon.sso.test.cba/identity). Pass this as base_url. This is the simplest approach and works for standard-compliant providers.

Option B: Use openid_configuration_uri. If the provider uses a non-standard discovery path, pass the full URL as openid_configuration_uri and derive the base_url from the issuer field in the fetched document. This handles edge cases.

Recommendation: Implement Option A as the default with a fallback to Option B. When the customer provides a URL, try stripping /.well-known/openid-configuration. If it doesn’t end with that suffix, treat the full URL as a custom openid_configuration_uri.
When a customer provides a discovery URL, we should validate it before saving:

Fetch the document and verify it returns valid JSON.
Check that required fields are present: issuer, authorization_endpoint, token_endpoint, jwks_uri.
Verify that the issuer in the document matches the base URL (OIDC spec requirement).
Check that token_endpoint_auth_methods_supported includes a method we can use (client_secret_basic or client_secret_post).
Verify id_token_signing_alg_values_supported includes RS256 or our configured algorithm.
Optionally test-fetch the JWKS endpoint to confirm key availability.
Some corporate IdPs may not fully comply with OIDC Discovery spec (missing fields, non-standard paths).
Validate on save; allow manual overrides for advanced fields.
If the IdP’s discovery endpoint goes down, SSO login breaks because Assent fetches it on each auth flow.
Cache the discovery document with a TTL (e.g. 1 hour). Assent supports openid_configuration passthrough.
If we cache JWKS too aggressively, we may reject tokens signed with newly rotated keys.
On JWT verification failure, refetch JWKS once before returning an error.
The issuer in the discovery doc must match the URL we fetched from. If not, it could indicate a misconfiguration or attack.
Strict issuer matching on validation. Reject mismatches.
The customer’s IdP may only support client_secret_post but Assent defaults to client_secret_basic.
Read token_endpoint_auth_methods_supported from discovery doc and auto-select, or let customer override.

Custom OIDC SSO Strategy
Preliminary Research & Sprint Stories
March 2026
1. Executive Summary
This document covers the preliminary research for adding a "Custom" OIDC SSO strategy to our platform. Currently, customers must choose from pre-configured providers (Google, Azure AD, etc.) where endpoints are hardcoded in the respective Assent strategy modules. The goal is to let customers provide their own OpenID Connect discovery URL and have our system auto-configure all necessary endpoints, signing keys, and authentication parameters from it.
Key outcome: A customer like CBA can supply a URL such as https://signon.sso.test.cba/identity/.well-known/openid-configuration and our platform will automatically discover and configure the authorization endpoint, token endpoint, JWKS URI, and supported scopes/claims.
2. Background: OIDC Discovery
2.1 What is .well-known/openid-configuration?
Every OpenID Connect-compliant identity provider publishes a JSON document at a standardized URL path (/.well-known/openid-configuration). This document contains all the metadata a relying party needs to integrate: authorization endpoint, token endpoint, JWKS URI, supported scopes, supported signing algorithms, and more.
Instead of hardcoding these values per provider, an application can fetch this single document and extract everything it needs. This is the foundation of OIDC auto-discovery and is defined by the OpenID Connect Discovery 1.0 specification (extended by RFC 8414).
2.2 Key Fields in the Discovery Document
The discovery document is a flat JSON object. The most critical fields for our integration are:


2.3 Example: Google Discovery Document (abbreviated)
{ "issuer": "https://accounts.google.com",
  "authorization_endpoint": "https://accounts.google.com/o/oauth2/v2/auth",
  "token_endpoint": "https://oauth2.googleapis.com/token",
  "jwks_uri": "https://www.googleapis.com/oauth2/v3/certs",
  "scopes_supported": ["openid", "email", "profile"],
  "id_token_signing_alg_values_supported": ["RS256"] }
3. Integration with Assent (POC Findings)
3.1 Current Architecture
We currently use Assent v0.3.1 for SSO. Each provider (Google, Azure AD, etc.) is a dedicated module that uses Assent.Strategy.OIDC.Base and hardcodes a base_url in its default_config/1 callback. Assent then appends /.well-known/openid-configuration to that base URL, fetches the discovery document, and uses it to drive the authorize and callback flows.
Key insight: Assent.Strategy.OIDC already supports full OIDC auto-discovery natively. The strategy accepts a base_url config option and fetches the discovery document at runtime. This means we do not need to write custom HTTP fetching or endpoint-parsing logic. We simply need to pass the customer’s issuer URL as the base_url.
3.2 Relevant Assent Configuration Options
The Assent.Strategy.OIDC module accepts several config keys that are relevant to our custom strategy:
base_url (required) — The OIDC issuer URL. Assent appends the well-known path to this.
openid_configuration_uri (optional) — Override the default discovery path. Defaults to /.well-known/openid-configuration.
openid_configuration (optional) — Pass the full discovery document directly (skip the HTTP fetch). Useful for testing or caching.
client_authentication_method (optional) — Defaults to client_secret_basic. Can be: none, client_secret_basic, client_secret_post, client_secret_jwt, private_key_jwt.
id_token_signed_response_alg (optional) — Expected signing algorithm for ID tokens. Defaults to RS256.
trusted_audiences (optional) — List of audiences considered valid when verifying the ID token.
3.3 POC Approach: Using Assent.Strategy.OIDC Directly
The simplest and most maintainable approach is to use Assent.Strategy.OIDC directly (or wrap it minimally) rather than creating a full custom strategy module. The configuration would be assembled dynamically from the customer’s stored settings:
# Dynamic config built from customer SSO settings:
[
  client_id: customer_sso.client_id,
  client_secret: customer_sso.client_secret,
  base_url: customer_sso.openid_configuration_url |> extract_issuer(),
  redirect_uri: "https://app.example.com/auth/sso/callback",
  authorization_params: [scope: "openid email profile"]
]
|> Assent.Strategy.OIDC.authorize_url()

3.4 Alternative: Custom OIDC.Base Strategy Module
If we want a cleaner abstraction and the ability to add custom normalization logic, we can define a dedicated module using Assent.Strategy.OIDC.Base:
defmodule MyApp.Strategy.CustomOIDC do
  use Assent.Strategy.OIDC.Base

  @impl true
  def default_config(config) do
    # base_url is injected at runtime from customer settings
    [base_url: Keyword.get(config, :base_url)]
  end

  @impl true
  def normalize(_config, user) do
    {:ok, %{
      "sub" => user["sub"],
      "email" => user["email"],
      "name" => user["name"]
    }}
  end
end

Recommendation: Start with the direct Assent.Strategy.OIDC approach for the POC. If normalization requirements emerge during testing with real customer IdPs (e.g. CBA), extract into a custom OIDC.Base module.
3.5 Handling the Discovery URL vs. Issuer URL
Customers will provide a full discovery URL like:
https://signon.sso.test.cba/identity/.well-known/openid-configuration
But Assent expects a base_url (the issuer) and appends the well-known path itself. We have two options:
Option A: Strip the well-known suffix. Parse the provided URL and extract the base (e.g. https://signon.sso.test.cba/identity). Pass this as base_url. This is the simplest approach and works for standard-compliant providers.
Option B: Use openid_configuration_uri. If the provider uses a non-standard discovery path, pass the full URL as openid_configuration_uri and derive the base_url from the issuer field in the fetched document. This handles edge cases.
Recommendation: Implement Option A as the default with a fallback to Option B. When the customer provides a URL, try stripping /.well-known/openid-configuration. If it doesn’t end with that suffix, treat the full URL as a custom openid_configuration_uri.
3.6 Validation Considerations
When a customer provides a discovery URL, we should validate it before saving:
Fetch the document and verify it returns valid JSON.
Check that required fields are present: issuer, authorization_endpoint, token_endpoint, jwks_uri.
Verify that the issuer in the document matches the base URL (OIDC spec requirement).
Check that token_endpoint_auth_methods_supported includes a method we can use (client_secret_basic or client_secret_post).
Verify id_token_signing_alg_values_supported includes RS256 or our configured algorithm.
Optionally test-fetch the JWKS endpoint to confirm key availability.
4. Risks & Considerations


5. Proposed Sprint Stories
The following stories are proposed for refinement in the next sprint. They are ordered to build incrementally.













6. Summary & Next Steps
Total estimated effort: 29 story points across 6 stories. SSO-101 through SSO-103 form the MVP; SSO-104 through SSO-106 add resilience, flexibility, and validation.

Recommended next steps:
Refine the above stories with the team in sprint planning.
Confirm CBA discovery endpoint availability and obtain test credentials.
Build a minimal POC using Assent.Strategy.OIDC with a test IdP (Keycloak/Auth0) to validate the approach before committing to full stories.
Align with the frontend team on the "Custom" option UI for the SSO configuration page.
The following stories are proposed for refinement in the next sprint.
They are ordered to build incrementally.
Add "Custom" option to SSO provider selection
As an admin configuring SSO, I want to see a "Custom" option in the SSO provider dropdown (alongside Google, Azure AD, etc.) so that I can configure SSO for any OIDC-compliant identity provider.

A "Custom" option appears in the SSO provider selection list.
When "Custom" is selected, a text field for "OpenID Configuration URL" is displayed.
The URL field includes placeholder text showing the expected format (e.g. https://idp.example.com/.well-known/openid-configuration).
Selecting a different provider hides the custom URL field.
Validate and store custom OIDC discovery URL

Description: As an admin configuring SSO, I want to see a "Custom" option in the SSO provider dropdown (alongside Google, Azure AD, etc.) so that I can configure SSO for any OIDC-compliant identity provider.

As the system, when an admin provides a custom OpenID Configuration URL, I want to validate the URL by fetching the discovery document and checking required fields, so that misconfigured URLs are caught early.

On save, the system fetches the provided URL and parses the JSON response.
Validation passes only if issuer, authorization_endpoint, token_endpoint, and jwks_uri are present.
The system verifies the issuer field matches the base URL derived from the provided discovery URL.
Clear error messages are shown for: unreachable URL, invalid JSON, missing required fields, issuer mismatch.
On successful validation, the discovery URL (and optionally the cached document) is stored with the SSO configuration.
Implement Custom OIDC authorize/callback flow using Assent

Description: As the system, when an admin provides a custom OpenID Configuration URL, I want to validate the URL by fetching the discovery document and checking required fields, so that misconfigured URLs are caught early.

clipboardHistoryRecords.text: As the system, when a user initiates SSO login for a tenant configured with a custom OIDC provider, I want to use the stored discovery URL to drive the Assent.Strategy.OIDC authorize and callback flow so that users can authenticate against any OIDC-compliant IdP.

The authorize phase builds an Assent.Strategy.OIDC config using the stored base_url, client_id, and client_secret.
The user is redirected to the authorization_endpoint discovered from the provider’s configuration.
The callback phase exchanges the auth code at the discovered token_endpoint.
The ID token is validated using keys fetched from the discovered jwks_uri.
User claims (sub, email, name) are extracted and mapped to our internal user model.
Session is established on successful authentication.
Cache OIDC discovery document for resilience
As the system, I want to cache the OIDC discovery document so that SSO login does not fail if the provider’s discovery endpoint is temporarily unavailable.

After successful validation (SSO-102), the discovery document JSON is stored alongside the SSO configuration.
During the auth flow, the cached document is passed via Assent’s openid_configuration config option.
A background job refreshes the cached document periodically (e.g. every hour).
If a refresh fails, the stale cache is retained and an alert is logged.
On JWKS verification failure, a one-time refetch of the JWKS is attempted before returning an error.
As an admin, I want optional fields to override auto-detected settings (client auth method, signing algorithm, custom scopes) for IdPs that require non-default configuration.

Optional fields are shown under an "Advanced Settings" toggle: Client Authentication Method (dropdown), ID Token Signing Algorithm (dropdown), Custom Scopes (text input).
Dropdowns are pre-populated with values from the discovery document when available.
If no overrides are set, sensible defaults are used (client_secret_basic, RS256, openid email profile).
Overrides are passed through to the Assent config and take precedence over discovery document values.
End-to-end testing with CBA (and other real IdPs)
As the team, we want to test the custom OIDC flow with CBA’s identity provider (and optionally other real IdPs) to validate the implementation works with enterprise-grade providers.

Configure SSO using the CBA discovery URL (once the endpoint is live).
Successfully complete the full authorize → callback → user creation flow.
Verify token validation with CBA’s signing keys.
Document any provider-specific quirks or workarounds required.
If CBA endpoint is not yet available, test with a local Keycloak or Auth0 instance as a stand-in.
Total estimated effort: 29 story points across 6 stories

**Key insight:** `Assent.Strategy.OIDC` already supports full OIDC auto-discovery natively. The strategy accepts a ***base_url*** config option and fetches the discovery document at runtime. This means we do **not** need to write custom HTTP fetching or endpoint-parsing logic. We simply need to pass the customer’s issuer URL as the base_url.

The key takeaway from the research: **Assent already supports OIDC auto-discovery natively**. The `Assent.Strategy.OIDC` module accepts a `base_url` config and fetches the `.well-known/openid-configuration` document at runtime — so we don't need custom HTTP fetching or endpoint-parsing logic. We just need to pipe the customer's issuer URL into the existing config.

Key insight: Assent.Strategy.OIDC already provides built-in support for full OIDC auto-discovery. By supplying the customer’s issuer URL via the base_url configuration option, the strategy automatically retrieves the .well-known/openid-configuration document at runtime. This eliminates the need for any custom HTTP fetching or manual endpoint parsing—we simply pass the issuer URL through the existing configuration.
The strategy accepts a base_url config option and fetches the discovery document at runtime.
This means we do not need to write custom HTTP fetching or endpoint-parsing logic. We simply need to pass the customer’s issuer URL as the base_url.

notes.text:
Welcome to Raycast Notes

Thank you for checking out Raycast Notes, a handy small scratchpad for your new ideas, meeting notes, and things you want to jot down quickly.



📑 Browsing Notes

You can find your notes with ⌘ P or via the panel button in the title bar. You can have up to 5 notes on the free plan, and an unlimited amount with Raycast Pro.

📏 Window Resizing

Raycast Notes’ height is dynamic by default. You can override this by simply re-sizing it manually.

To get back to auto-sizing, you can hover around the bottom edge, where you will see an “auto-size” button appear.

⚡ Actions

You can find some helpful actions from the ⌘ K menu or the ⌘ icon in the title bar.

🎨 Formatting

You can use markdown to format your text, using # for headings, **bold**, [] for task items and so on. Check out the full list here.



We hope you enjoy using Raycast Notes. Please feel free to leave us feedback in our Slack Community ❤️
